speaker: interviewer
text: --the events that happened from the years of 1943 to '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The main thing what I am trying to tell you is actually how the farmer that accepted me found out that I was a Jew. And that's-- in my life, I think it's a very important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here, I'm disguising myself as a Polack. And I'm living as a Polish kid until this Jewish guy comes to this farmer, which was a friend of his. They were actually knowing each other from the war-- before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looks at me, and he says that I'm a Jew. And he kind of opened his eyes, this young farmer. And I heard all this conversation. And he says, no, I am sent by the-- by his brother from the hospital. No Jew can actually live in that hospital for a few weeks. And he cannot be a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, this Jewish guy got some food from this farmer-- which was probably a decent guy-- and comes over to me. And he says, you know, what he says, that you are a Jew? And I tried to convince him that I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he pulled my pants off. And he identified me that I was a Jew. And he said to me that I have to let you go because I'm-- it was too dangerous to keep a Jew because he was risking his own life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, I'm going to have to let you go. So here, I am ready to take my few things that I have and leave. And he says, well, it's already going to the evening, why don't you spend the night over here? And the following day you, you leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the following day was actually a-- every once in awhile, the Germans would make, like, a sweep of the area. There was a lot of Russian prisoners and Jews that were wandering around. So they would make a sweep over the fields and the farms. And whoever was undesirable-- or didn't have any documents-- they would, of course, kill outright there and then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here in the morning, I'm ready to leave, and I see these people approaching the farm. And I'm grabbing my bundle. I want to run.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, no, don't run, because if you run they're going to kill you right there and then. You stay put, and you'll probably make it. I don't know how he took a chance of that. And they came. And they started to ask questions, if we've seen any Russian prisoners or any Jews. But they wouldn't recognize me that I was a Jew. So they asked if we seen anybody. And, of course, everybody said, no, we didn't see anybody. They walked right by. And they continued walking. And they didn't recognize me. But the guy saved my life at that point. And right after that, I wanted to leave. He says no, you stay a little bit longer till everything quiets down. And then you're going to go. And that went week after week, month after month. And I remained there for two years at this farm. The war ended, actually, for us because the Russians liberated us in 1944, summertime. And he's supposed to go into the army because he was a young man-- him and his brother. And I supposed to take care of the farm. So he said to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: This man--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to interrupt you.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Slepowronski, Mieczyslaw [NON-ENGLISH] Slepowronski. And his brother's name is Heniek Slepowronski. So this fellow who told me, listen, I saved your life. Now you've got to do something for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to, both of us go now. And his brother didn't know that I was Jewish, nor did his mother. And he had a sister. He did not tell that to anybody. He kept that secret all to himself-- continued keeping me over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was one of the farm boys. I was one of the help. And I was accepted by all the farmers. They knew that I was a, a-- I, I, I was a kid that had nobody. I come from Warsaw. My story seemed-- was holding pretty good. And this went through. And he's supposed to go to the army-- which he didn't go to the army. But I had to remain on the farm. I was not able to leave because they were in the partisans. And they knew all the-- all the roads and all the tricks. And everything was controlled until 1946. In 1946, I insisted that I've got to leave, and I've got to come to Warsaw. He finally let me go. Well, he was afraid that I'm going to really spill the beans on them. And I am not going to be the way he expected me, but I'm going to go and squeal on everybody that I know, that they were from the Home Army, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to remain until the end of '46 over there almost. In the Home Army--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and with them at the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the area, on the farm over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the Home Army with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was part of them, but I was not really in the actions where they were taking part of it, because they were killing Jews, and they were killing Russians. And they were controlling the entire area of the city of Sokolow Podlaski and Sieldce, the whole area. They were combing the city-- they were the police, and they were God, and they were judge, and they were everything. They made people do whatever they wanted. And they were pretty good at it. And the communists had really very little to say because they couldn't even come in and control them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I came back-- after the war, I came back. And I came back to the house that I lived in Warsaw, on Radzyminska. And I looked up my neighbors. And they were happy to see me, and all that. But I did not find anybody from my family. I went to the Jewish kamitet, and I looked up the names for survivors who survived, who did not. There was nobody from my family that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back to the neighbors. And I told them that I don't want to be a Jew anymore. And during the years being on the farm, I became a, a Christian. I, I was able to get indoctrinated as a Christian. I even-- a farmer put-- how would you say, he Christianed me-- over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which-- I believe that this was a good thing. And I will not die as one without being Christian. I wanted to be a Christian. I did not want to be a Jew. And I had nothing to do, at that point, with any Jew. And when I came to Warsaw, I tried to get myself into a home for children that had nothing to do with Jews and there were no Jews over there. So I found a home. And I told the director that I'm a Jew. And I want to be a Christian, and I want to live among Christians, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she baptized me with the priest. And I was sent to a, a-- an orphanage that was run by Jesuits in Poland. It was a, a monastery. I was there for awhile, for about three, four months.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, on the farm, in the village, I met another Jewish boy that we didn't know from each other for a year. He didn't know that I was Jewish. And he didn't know that I was Jewish because it was too dangerous to disclose that to anybody. But at one point, I found out that he was a Jewish boy. And we kept a friendship. When I came to, to this-- to this orphanage, that I told this director that I wanted to be a Christian and all that, I wrote to my friend, and I told him all about it. And he left the farm, and he joined me. And both of us went into this monastery. And we lived in this monastery, I think, for about three months or so. After being there for three months, I realized that this is not my cup of tea-- because somehow, I was a Jew in heart, and I wanted to go back to Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided that I gotta get out from there. But I was afraid to disclose it over there. I, I was paranoia all the time, from the war, I suppose. So anyway, I went away from there, and, and I came to Warsaw. I came to Warsaw, and I went into-- to the Jews, to the kamitet. And I told them all about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got into an orphanage in Warsaw that brought me into another orphanage in another city of Lodz. And I met over there a, a director of this orphanage that really made me a Jew. Because without him, I'd probably go back to the monastery. I could not-- I could not acclimatize myself to the Jewish life. Though there were people not religious or anything like that, they were mostly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people like me. But they were from a different background. They were older, and all that. Anyway, this director, his name was Pavel Hirshberg. And we met over there. They called him in. And he brought me into Judaism. He introduced me to a group of kids that were my kind of kids, [SOBBING] that survived the war just like I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [SOBBING] my friend and I came in, and we lived with these people for quite awhile. So, we went to school. And we managed to get ourselves on the right track as far as Jews. I kind of gave up my, my belief in Christianity. And I'd stopped-- I stopped believing in Jesus Christ. I-- since then, I'm, I'm a secular Jew. I'm a Jew by tradition. I am not a religious Jew. But nevertheless, I am a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's part, you know, of my-- the end of the war years, I went to school. And I remained like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to after Poland, after Warsaw--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, after Warsaw--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or, or Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's say in the later years, I went to school. And I worked. And I came-- in 1953, I came into Belgium. I made my way out of Poland, and I came into Belgium. I came into Belgium. And I started to work in Belgium as an adult already. I was able to get a temporary stay in Belgium as a refugee from Poland. And I signed up for the United States and Canada. Whoever would take me in, I wanted to build some kind of a future for myself. And with time, I was called into the American Consulate in Belgium. And I was able to emigrate to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1955, November-- winter. So I came into the United States. I had an affidavit signed by a Jew in Richmond, Virginia-- I don't remember his name. But he was very nice, that he was able to bring me to the United States. I came in. And I looked him up in Richmond, Virginia. And I wanted to thank him, that he doesn't owe me anything. And it just so happened that he didn't really want me to, to do any work for him. He's supposed to be some kind of a industrialist. It was just a, a formality. So he was probably more scared that I shouldn't lean on his back, like I was scared that I have to work over there. Anyway, we understood each other. I stayed in Richmond, Virginia, for about a month. And I came to New York City. Came to New York City, I rented a small furnished apartment. And I was, by trade, a carpenter. I got myself a job as a carpenter. And I started to build my future slowly. Then I met a girl, an American Jewish girl from Brooklyn. We got married in 1956 in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Janet. Her maiden name was [? Greenberg. ?] And we got married. And we had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she gave birth to a boy, Jeffrey, in 1959. And we lived a nice, happy life for awhile. And over the years, being in New York, I did not like to live in New York, and work-- especially my trade, I was a construction worker. And it was cold. And I couldn't stand this kind of a life, this kind of world. I wanted to be more independent. And I decided that I am going to move to Florida. We went to Florida a few times on vacation. And I decided that this would be the right place to go. Maybe in Florida, I would be able to maybe do some building on my own, and become independent, and so on. This didn't work out. But we lived in Florida for about four years. I was able to save some money, bought a house over there. Anyway, she decided not to live in Florida. She did not like Florida. So from Florida, she thought I would get back to New York. But I didn't want to go back to New York. We moved to California. We moved to California. And in California, after a short time, I got a contractor-- general contractor's license. And I became a independent contractor. So I've worked here for-- since 1977, I worked as a general contractor. The name of the company that I formed was [INAUDIBLE] Construction. I was not a very large contractor. I was a small contractor. I had people working for me. I was happy for awhile, but my wife was never happy to be in California. She always resented it. She always nagged on me-- and to the point that we were trying to get a divorce many, many years ago, when we just got to California. And life was going up and down, one day better, one day worse. But life was not good. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: --finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your separate ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: --went separate ways. And I met-- I used to go to Israel to see-- because my friends that I had in Europe went to Israel. And I always kept in contact with them. And I decided I'm going to go and visit them. And I also had an uncle that I found later on. And actually, I found three uncles that were-- they ran away to Russia in 1939. And then I found out that they were all living in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see them, and we made contact. And from there on-- that was, like, 1962. And from there on, every few years, I would go to Israel and see my uncles. I have one cousin. And I see my friends. And over and over, over the years, I kept in contact with everybody. And one of my visits in Israel, a mutual friend introduced a girl named Ella. She was a widow. And we met. And after-- took us a few years to get to know each other because I lived here, and she lived there. She was traveling, and I was traveling to see each other. And we finally decided that we were going to get married. And we got married a year ago. And we are very happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll get to meet Ella in a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Ella in Israel when one my friends decided to go on a picnic. And this was a mutual friend, so they invited Ella to this picnic. And they didn't tell her anything. And, of course, I was also with my friends. And we met at this picnic, not thinking of anything. We kind of started to talk to each other. She gave me her phone number, and the number-- phone number of her son that lives in LA. And I looked him up when I came back to the-- to the United States. And I made contact with him. And then a short while later, Ella came to see her son. And of course, she saw me. And we kind of got to know each other a little bit better. And then I went to Israel and I stayed over there for a few weeks. And slowly, we kind of spent more and more time together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a grandchild, which actually I adopted-- not legally, but in my heart, I adopted him. His name-- he-- my son married a widow. So the grandchild actually is my, my daughter-in-law's child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Joshua-- Joshua Keating. He doesn't go on my son's name because they are not legally-- he is not legally adopted. So he lives in Arizona with my son. My son lives in Phoenix, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go quite often-- when I'm in this country, I go quite often to see him. But my life is kind of split at this point between Israel and the United States. I still come here for a few months. I love the United States. [CRYING] And I kind of feel that I cannot just abandon it and say, OK, I live in Israel today. I love Israel. I live in Israel. I love everybody there. But I still kind of miss the States. And I like to come here and live here for a few months. So that's what I'm doing at this point. What's going to be in the future, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, do you have some kind of message, or something that you would like to tell, maybe Joshua, or future generations, anybody that might be watching your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to give one message to everybody-- just not to discriminate against anybody, be open-minded, not to make harsh decisions. Digest everything what people tell you, and what the government tells you, and make your own decisions. But the main thing-- not to hate anybody, not to hold a grudge against anybody. Live, forgive, and try to live in peace. That's my message to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody should really live the way he wants-- free, not to be influenced by a religion, or anything like that-- just be a free man and do his own thinking. Not anybody should think for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Henry, can you please introduce this wonderful woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my wife, Ella-- that we are married for over a year right now. But we know each other already for about five years. We are very happy. And what more can I say? She's a wonderful person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ella, is there anything that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a very happy woman. I have Henry, my, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
